title: Brand as Asset

content.statement: Brand compounds trust; trust compounds sales.
content.question: What part of your brand are you compromising for short-term gain?
sop: Write down the one thing you want to be instantly recognized for.
Audit your last 10 public touchpoints (posts, emails, calls) for consistency with that one thing.
Rate your Recognition, Trust, and Consistency each 1-10 (see calculation, page 5) based on honest customer feedback, not your own opinion.
Fix the lowest-scoring variable first, it's dragging your brand equity down the most.
Protect brand consistency even when a shortcut would be more profitable this month.
questions: What part of your brand are you compromising for short-term gain?
Could a stranger describe what you stand for in one sentence, or does it change depending on who's asking?
What's one inconsistency between what you promise publicly and what you actually deliver?

definitions.def: The premium value your reputation adds to your product, letting you charge more or convert faster than an identical unbranded competitor.

content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10: Recognition, Trust, Consistency. Recognition 7 times Trust 8 times Consistency 6 equals 336. Higher means your brand quietly sells for you and supports premium pricing; lower means you're competing on price alone.
content.closing_statement: Trust compounds slower than it breaks, guard it.

caption:
Principle Nº 173: Brand as Asset

Brand compounds trust; trust compounds sales.

Ask yourself: What part of your brand are you compromising for short-term gain?
